annotations.label: Integer factorization

anchor.snippet: integer factorization is the decomposition of a positive integer into a product of integers

annotations.note: `Nat.primeFactorsList n` produces the list of prime factors of a positive integer, directly formalizing the decomposition concept.

annotations.label: Composite and prime numbers

anchor.snippet: Every positive integer greater than 1 is either the product of two or more integer factors greater than 1

annotations.note: `Nat.Prime` is defined but there is no dedicated `Nat.Composite` predicate; compositeness must be expressed as `¬ Nat.Prime n ∧ 1 < n`.

annotations.label: 15 composite, 7 prime

anchor.snippet: 15 is a composite number because

annotations.note: Specific worked numerical example of compositeness/primality, not stated as a lemma in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Factorization of 60

anchor.snippet: for example 60 = 3 · 20 = 3 · (5 · 4)

annotations.note: Illustrative numerical example, not present as a named result in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Prime factorization theorem

anchor.snippet: the result is always unique up to the order of the factors by the prime factorization theorem

annotations.note: `Nat.primeFactorsList_unique` gives uniqueness up to permutation of the prime factorization.

annotations.label: Trial division

anchor.snippet: the simplest method is trial division : checking if the number is divisible by prime numbers

annotations.note: Grep for `trialDivision`/`trial_division` returned no matches in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Semiprimes (hardest instances)

anchor.snippet: The hardest instances of these problems (for currently known techniques) are semiprimes

annotations.note: No `Semiprime` predicate is defined anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of arithmetic

anchor.snippet: By the fundamental theorem of arithmetic , every positive integer has a unique prime factorization

annotations.note: Existence and uniqueness are given by `Nat.prod_primeFactorsList` together with `Nat.primeFactorsList_unique`; the general form lives as `UniqueFactorizationMonoid`.

annotations.label: Trial division on n = 171·p·q

anchor.snippet: if n = 171 × p × q where p < q are very large primes, trial division will quickly produce the factors 3 and 19

annotations.note: Algorithmic anecdote, not represented as a Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: Fermat's method on 13729·1372933·18848997161

anchor.snippet: if n is the product of the primes 13729 , 1372933 , and 18848997161

annotations.note: Specific numerical example of Fermat's factorization method; Mathlib has no Fermat factorization algorithm.

annotations.label: RSA-240 factorization

anchor.snippet: a 240-digit (795-bit) number ( RSA-240 ) was factored by a team of researchers

annotations.note: Historical computational record; no `RSA` decl in Mathlib.

annotations.label: RSA-250 factorization

anchor.snippet: The largest such semiprime yet factored was RSA-250 , an 829-bit number with 250 decimal digits

annotations.note: Historical computational record; not formalized.

annotations.label: No known polynomial-time factoring algorithm

anchor.snippet: No algorithm has been published that can factor all integers in polynomial time

annotations.note: Open meta-mathematical statement about complexity; not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: GNFS sub-exponential running time

anchor.snippet: the algorithm with best theoretical asymptotic running time is the general number field sieve

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the general number field sieve or its L-notation runtime.

annotations.label: Shor's algorithm complexity

anchor.snippet: Shor's algorithm takes only O( b 3 ) time and O( b ) space on b -bit number inputs

annotations.note: Quantum complexity result; no Shor's algorithm formalization in Mathlib (the 'Shor' grep hits were 'ShortComplex').

annotations.label: Factorization in NP and co-NP

anchor.snippet: It is known to be in both NP and co-NP , meaning that both "yes" and "no" answers can be verified in polynomial time

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formal complexity-theoretic classes NP/coNP.

annotations.label: Factorization in UP and co-UP

anchor.snippet: shows that the problem is in both UP and co-UP

annotations.note: Complexity classes UP/coUP are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Factorization in BQP

anchor.snippet: It is known to be in BQP because of Shor's algorithm

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the quantum complexity class BQP.

annotations.label: Primality in polynomial time (AKS)

anchor.snippet: The composite/prime problem can be solved in polynomial time (in the number b of digits of n ) with the AKS primality test

annotations.note: Grep for `AKS`/`aks_primality` returned no Mathlib matches.

annotations.label: Decision problem (Integer factorization)
annotations.note: Decision-problem framing belongs to complexity theory, which Mathlib does not formalize.

annotations.label: Category 1 (special-purpose) algorithms

anchor.snippet: An important subclass of special-purpose factoring algorithms is the Category 1 or First Category algorithms

annotations.note: Algorithmic taxonomy not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: General-purpose (Category 2) algorithms

anchor.snippet: A general-purpose factoring algorithm, also known as a Category 2 , Second Category , or Kraitchik family algorithm

annotations.note: Algorithmic taxonomy not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Heuristic L-notation running time

anchor.snippet: there are many integer factoring algorithms that heuristically have expected running time

annotations.note: L-notation and heuristic runtimes of factoring algorithms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Schnorr–Seysen–Lenstra rigorous running time

anchor.snippet: The Schnorr–Seysen–Lenstra probabilistic algorithm has been rigorously proven by Lenstra and Pomerance

annotations.note: Schnorr–Seysen–Lenstra factorization is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Class group G_Δ of binary quadratic forms

anchor.snippet: The algorithm uses the class group of positive binary quadratic forms of discriminant

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `ClassGroup` for Dedekind domains but no `BinaryQuadraticForm` class group of discriminant Δ.
